comment_text.sn20.9:1.3: For this Sutta and the next see the five dangers of fraternizing too much with families (AN 5.226 = Pvr 7:67.11).
comment_text.sn20.9:3.1: The same parable is given of Devadatta at Kd 17:4.5.1.

root_text.sn20.9:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn20.9:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena aññataro navo bhikkhu ativelaṁ kulāni upasaṅkamati.
root_text.sn20.9:1.7: “ime hi nāma therā bhikkhū kulāni upasaṅkamitabbaṁ maññissanti, kimaṅgaṁ panāhan”ti?
root_text.sn20.9:2.1: Atha kho sambahulā bhikkhū yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinnā kho te bhikkhū bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn20.9:2.2: “idha, bhante, aññataro navo bhikkhu ativelaṁ kulāni upasaṅkamati.
root_text.sn20.9:2.6: ‘ime hi nāma therā bhikkhū kulāni upasaṅkamitabbaṁ maññissanti, kimaṅgaṁ panāhan’”ti.
root_text.sn20.9:3.3: Te taṁ sarasiṁ ogāhetvā soṇḍāya bhisamuḷālaṁ abbuhetvā suvikkhālitaṁ vikkhāletvā akaddamaṁ saṅkhāditvā ajjhoharanti.
root_text.sn20.9:3.4: Tesaṁ taṁ vaṇṇāya ceva hoti balāya ca, na ca tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchanti maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn20.9:3.5: Tesaṁyeva kho pana, bhikkhave, mahānāgānaṁ anusikkhamānā taruṇā bhiṅkacchāpā taṁ sarasiṁ ogāhetvā soṇḍāya bhisamuḷālaṁ abbuhetvā na suvikkhālitaṁ vikkhāletvā sakaddamaṁ asaṅkhāditvā ajjhoharanti.
root_text.sn20.9:3.6: Tesaṁ taṁ neva vaṇṇāya hoti na balāya.
root_text.sn20.9:3.7: Tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchanti maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn20.9:4.1: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, idha therā bhikkhū pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya gāmaṁ vā nigamaṁ vā piṇḍāya pavisanti.
root_text.sn20.9:4.4: Te taṁ lābhaṁ agadhitā amucchitā anajjhopannā ādīnavadassāvino nissaraṇapaññā paribhuñjanti.
root_text.sn20.9:4.5: Tesaṁ taṁ vaṇṇāya ceva hoti balāya ca, na ca tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchanti maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn20.9:4.6: Tesaṁyeva kho pana, bhikkhave, therānaṁ bhikkhūnaṁ anusikkhamānā navā bhikkhū pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya gāmaṁ vā nigamaṁ vā piṇḍāya pavisanti.
root_text.sn20.9:4.9: Te taṁ lābhaṁ gadhitā mucchitā ajjhopannā anādīnavadassāvino anissaraṇapaññā paribhuñjanti.
root_text.sn20.9:4.10: Tesaṁ taṁ neva vaṇṇāya hoti na balāya, te tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchanti maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn20.9:4.12: ‘agadhitā amucchitā anajjhopannā ādīnavadassāvino nissaraṇapaññā taṁ lābhaṁ paribhuñjissāmā’ti.

translation_text.sn20.9:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn20.9:1.3: Now at that time a certain junior mendicant went to visit families too often.
translation_text.sn20.9:1.5: “Venerable, don’t go to visit families too often.”
translation_text.sn20.9:1.6: But that mendicant, when spoken to by the mendicants, said this,
translation_text.sn20.9:1.7: “But these senior mendicants think they can go to visit families, so why can’t I?”
translation_text.sn20.9:2.1: And then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what had happened. The Buddha said:
translation_text.sn20.9:3.1: “Once upon a time, mendicants, there was a great lake in the jungle,
translation_text.sn20.9:3.3: They’d plunge into the lake and pull up lotus bulbs with their trunks. They’d wash them thoroughly until they were free of mud before chewing and swallowing them.
translation_text.sn20.9:3.4: That was good for their appearance and strength, and wouldn’t result in death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn20.9:3.5: The young cubs, following the example of the great bull elephants, plunged into the lake and pulled up lotus bulbs with their trunks. But they didn’t wash them thoroughly, and while they were still muddy they chewed and swallowed them.
translation_text.sn20.9:3.6: That was not good for their appearance and strength, and resulted in death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.1: In the same way, there are senior mendicants who robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.2: There they speak on the teachings,
translation_text.sn20.9:4.3: and lay people demonstrate their confidence in them.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.4: And when they get things, they use them untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.5: That’s good for their appearance and strength, and doesn’t result in death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.6: Junior mendicants, following the example of the senior mendicants, robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter the town or village for alms.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.7: There they speak on the teachings,
translation_text.sn20.9:4.8: and lay people demonstrate their confidence in them.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.9: But when they get things, they use them tied, infatuated, attached, blind to the drawbacks, not understanding the escape.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.10: That’s not good for their appearance and strength, and results in death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn20.9:4.11: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn20.9:4.12: ‘When we get things, we will use them untied, uninfatuated, unattached, seeing the drawbacks, and understanding the escape.’
